coredata.dc:description: The plants were suffered from oxidative damage caused by arsenic stress, as a major environmental challenge posing a serious threat to both yield and quality of crops. To tackle this challenge, herein, iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) nanozyme, synthesized via one-step hydrothermal method, has been reported as a highly active reactive oxygen species targeted (ROS-targeted) scavenger. It effectively alleviated arsenic-induced oxidative stress in lettuce. Fe-CDs nanozyme possessed POD-like, CAT-like, and SOD-like enzymes activity, effectively scavenging a variety of ROS (H2O2, O2 •− and ·OH). Electrochemical tests fully validated that its excellent enzymatic activity was attributed to the acceleration of electron transfer and enhancement of catalytic efficiency. Furthermore, theoretical calculations confirmed that iron ions as the catalyst site enhanced the adsorption of Fe-CDs to H2O2 (−0.666 eV) better that of CDs (−0.171 eV), accelerating Fe-CDs nanozyme ROS-targeted scavenging. Based on this, the finding from using Fe-CDs to mitigate arsenic stress in lettuce revealed Fe-CDs nanozyme enabled to effectively exert the role of antioxidant nanozyme to clear excessive accumulated ROS in lettuce. This has successfully reduced the uptake of arsenic by lettuce (decreased arsenic content to 34.7 %), thereby enhancing the stress resistance of lettuce. Laser confocal image have revealed a reduction of 36 % in the average fluorescence intensity of ROS in leaves subsequent Fe-CDs treatment. It was observable that Fe-CDs could be used as a potential antioxidant in ROS scavenging system, via a nanozyme catalytic strategy. The accumulated ROS in biological systems was down-regulated, reducing the abiotic stress in crops consequently. This innovative strategy of designing a nanomaterialspecifically engineeredto targeted-scavenge the ROS system paves a novel avenue for effectively inhibiting plants’ uptake of arsenic.

The ability of Fe-CDs nanozyme to scavenge ROS. Steady-state kinetic analysis of (a) TMB substrate and (b) H2O2 substrate by Fe-CDs. (c) Typical kinetic curves of oxygen generation from the decomposition of H2O2 catalyzed. (d) Schematic diagram of Fe-CDs and CDs SOD-like activity to scavenge O2 •−. (e) •OH scavenging capacity of CDs and Fe-CDs was quantitative by UV method. (f) Schematic diagram of Fe-CDs and CDs •DPPH scavenge ability.

ESR spectra of (a) O2 •− and (b) •OH spin adduct were obtained to verify scavenging capacity of CDs and Fe-CDs. (c) CV and (d) EIS characterization for CDs and Fe-CDs. (f) The schematic diagram of electrochemical reaction of CDs/Fe-CDs in a three-electrode system.

DFT models of the adsorption of H2O2 on (a) CDs and (b) Fe-CDs substituted graphene oxide after structure optimization and the calculated adsorption energy of H2O2. The charge-density difference maps of (c) CDs and (d) Fe-CDs. Side view of ESP distribution of (e) CDs and (f) Fe-CDs, top view of ESP distribution of (g) CDs and (h) Fe-CDs.

(a) The growth of morphology lettuce. (b) arsenic content and (c) MDA content in lettuce leaves. (d) POD activity, (e) CAT activity, (f) SOD activity. Marked with different letters indicate a significant difference (p < 0.05).

ROS levels in leaf mesophyll cells monitored by confocal fluorescence microscopy images of DCF fluorescence intensity in the presence of Fe-CDs nanozyme. Scale bar: 25 μm. (a) The control group, (b) arsenic stress group, and (c) Fe- CDs treatment group. (d) Mean fluorescence intensity of different treatment. (e) Changes in the equilibrium relationship between antioxidant enzymes and ROS in biological systems.
Fe-CDs with multi-enzyme activity had application in scavenging ROS system in lettuce.

head.ce:title: Iron-doped carbon dots nanozyme scavenged reactive oxygen species system for inhibiting effectively the uptake of arsenic in lettuce

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The plants were suffered from oxidative damage caused by arsenic stress, as a major environmental challenge posing a serious threat to both yield and quality of crops. To tackle this challenge, herein, iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) nanozyme, synthesized via one-step hydrothermal method, has been reported as a highly active reactive oxygen species targeted (ROS-targeted) scavenger. It effectively alleviated arsenic-induced oxidative stress in lettuce. Fe-CDs nanozyme possessed POD-like, CAT-like, and SOD-like enzymes activity, effectively scavenging a variety of ROS (H2O2, O2 •− and ·OH). Electrochemical tests fully validated that its excellent enzymatic activity was attributed to the acceleration of electron transfer and enhancement of catalytic efficiency. Furthermore, theoretical calculations confirmed that iron ions as the catalyst site enhanced the adsorption of Fe-CDs to H2O2 (−0.666 eV) better that of CDs (−0.171 eV), accelerating Fe-CDs nanozyme ROS-targeted scavenging. Based on this, the finding from using Fe-CDs to mitigate arsenic stress in lettuce revealed Fe-CDs nanozyme enabled to effectively exert the role of antioxidant nanozyme to clear excessive accumulated ROS in lettuce. This has successfully reduced the uptake of arsenic by lettuce (decreased arsenic content to 34.7 %), thereby enhancing the stress resistance of lettuce. Laser confocal image have revealed a reduction of 36 % in the average fluorescence intensity of ROS in leaves subsequent Fe-CDs treatment. It was observable that Fe-CDs could be used as a potential antioxidant in ROS scavenging system, via a nanozyme catalytic strategy. The accumulated ROS in biological systems was down-regulated, reducing the abiotic stress in crops consequently. This innovative strategy of designing a nanomaterialspecifically engineeredto targeted-scavenge the ROS system paves a novel avenue for effectively inhibiting plants’ uptake of arsenic.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: It is widelyacknowledgedthat with the anticipated significant growth of the global population by 2050, there is an urgent need to elevate global agricultural productivity to over 70 % in order to meet the demands of this vast population for food and other agricultural products. Hence, it is a major challenge for producers around the world to ensure the quality and yield of crops [1,2]. Arsenic (As) is prevalent in nature, as a highly toxic non-metallic element, hindering this agricultural expansion and exerting serious toxic impacts on living systems [3]. Generally speaking, arsenic can indeed enter the ecosystem through various pathways, primarily including mining, industrial activities, excessive use of fertilizers, pesticides and etc. [4,5]. Groundwater contamination with As has been widely documented in various countries. Consequently, arsenic stress on plants during agricultural irrigation has become a concern. The presence of arsenic in either soil or irrigation water can cause it to accumulate in crop plants, potentially affecting their growth and posing health risks to consumers [6]. On the one hand, prolonged exposure to or ingestion of arsenic contaminants can harm multiple systems within the body, including the skin, nervous system, and digestive system, increase the risk of developing cancer, and potentially lead to chronic poisoning and a wide range of health issues [7]. On the other hand, the plants enable be induced to generate excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) under As stress, notably hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), superoxide anion radical (O2 •−) and Hydroxyl free radical (•OH). In fact, ROS are essential for maintaining biological homeostasis and facilitating cell signaling, and with their production and scavenging in plants being meticulously balanced under usual circumstances. Despite their crucial role in aerobic metabolism, ROS levels can become excessive nevertheless under As stress due to the disruption of balance. Once up-regulated ROS exceeds the antioxidant response of plant cells, the oxidative damage will appear, such as protein deformation, DNA damage and membrane lipid peroxidation, ultimately causing cell death and inhibiting plant growth, reducing the plant yield [8,9]. In conclusion, arsenic pollution, a prevalent abiotic stressor, poses significant threats to plant growth and development, ultimately impacting food safety. Once arsenic enters plants, it disrupts metabolism by forming bonds with the sulfhydryl groups of essential proteins and enzymes, thereby hindering their normal functions [10]. Currently, common treatments for arsenic contamination include chemical amelioration, agricultural practices, and bioremediation. Usually, chemical amelioration uses iron and aluminum salts to bind As, reducing its bioavailability. While it is simple and effective in the short term, this method necessitates continuousinputof chemical reagent, incurs high costs, and potentially impacts soil fertility [11]. Long-term agricultural measures, including converting rice paddies to dry land, conducting deep plowing, and applying manure, enhance soil conditions, albeit at a slower pace of achieving results [12]. Research indicates that inoculating arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi can boost plant photosynthesis and alleviate arsenic-induced damage to leaf cells. However, this bioremediation approach poses challenges, is time-consuming, and faces difficulties in being scaled up [13]. Therefore, it is of great importance to find an efficient solution to clear ROS for addressing As stress in plants, which is precisely why nanotechnology-based treatments have emerged. Compared to chemical methods, nano technology avoids the use of large amounts of chemical reagents, thereby reducing chemical pollution and ecological risks. Nanomaterial spraying technology offers faster response times and a broader range of applications compared to biological treatment. For example, foliar application of cerium dioxide (CeO2) nanoparticles can inhibit the transfer of As from the roots to the stems of plants and reduce As content within the plant body. Besides, CeO2 nanoparticlesenhance the plant's tolerance to As stress by regulating its photosynthesis and antioxidant system [14].

Nanozymes, defined as nanomaterials that exhibit enzyme-mimicking properties, are highly efficient catalysts with immense potential in antioxidant applications. In recent years, a number of chemical materials have been developed and aimed at scavenging excess ROS caused by abiotic stress in living organisms. Among emerging contenders, the innovative nano-catalytic approach leveraging nanozymes has garnered significant attention and promise, offering a novel strategy to mitigate oxidative damage. ROS accumulation in organisms is often involved in catalytic reactions, thus laying a deep foundation for nanozymes catalytic strategies [15,16]. Drawing upon their remarkable antioxidant catalytic capabilities, akin to those of enzymes such as peroxidase (POD), catalase (CAT), and superoxide dismutase (SOD), nanozymes offer a potent means to target and down-regulate the excessive accumulation of ROS under abnormal physiological conditions. This capability underscores their potential to mitigate oxidative stress and protect cells from damage. For instance, CeO2 with its dual enzyme-mimicking properties akin to SOD and CAT, serves as a highly effective anti-inflammatory agent, adept at eliminating harmful H2O2 and O2 •− [17]. In addition, Prussian blue (PB) was utilized as a potent ROS scavenger on account of its multiple antioxidant enzyme-like activities under physiological conditions to confer remarkable cellular protection [18]. Nevertheless, the utilization of nanozymes in plants is still restrictive, and the catalytic mechanism is still unclear. Consequently, the development of multi-enzyme mimics capable of simultaneously scavenging multiple ROS holds immense potential in nano-catalytic biological applications, yet it also poses a significant challenge. Carbon dots (CDs) is a zero-dimensional nanomaterial. Based on its small size (<10 nm) and high biocompatibility, CDs have more catalytic sites, exhibiting excellent catalytic performance [19]. It is noteworthy that carbon dot-based nanozymes possess the remarkable ability to readily traverse biological barriers and penetrate into plants, thereby enabling them to exert significant effects on plant growth and stress resistance. This characteristic underscores their immense potential in enhancing plant health and resilience against environmental stressors, offering a promising new frontier in agricultural biotechnology [20–22]. Further, the large specific surface area and space-limiting effects of CDs ensure a high volume load on the active metal [23]. The tight bond between CDs and metal makes sure good stability and strong internal interaction as catalysts, which greatly stabilizes the metallic active constituents and increases their catalytic efficiency as nanozymes. However, the limited electron transfer efficiency within the CDs structure restricts catalytic efficiency, which hinders its further application. Fortunately, this could be improved effectively by doping hetero atoms. It can not only transform the electronic environment inside the CDs, but also provide catalytic active sites to endow the CDs new characteristics or functions [24,25]. At present, although some literature has already applied nanozymes in the plant field to promote plant growth or alleviate plant stress, their functionalities remain relatively limited. Furthermore, in the realmof plant applications, there is a scarcity of targeted studies on the impact of enhancing electron transfer capability on the catalytic effectiveness of nanozymes [26]. Therefore, it is imperative to design a nanozyme with high electron transfer efficiency that specifically targets and scavenges ROS system, thereby promoting plant growth and alleviating plant stress.

Herein, iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) nanozyme was synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method. Fe-CDs revealed excellent multi-simulated enzyme activity (POD, CAT and SOD) (Scheme 1 ). Electrochemical tests revealed that Fe-doping could effectively expedite the electron transfer ability and promote the efficient catalysis of nanozyme. To conduct further research the effect of iron ion on CDs nanozyme, we used density functional theory to calculate. The results indicated that iron was capable of enhancing the catalytic efficiency of nanozyme. Additionally, the adsorption of H2O2 by Fe-CDs outperformed that by CDs significantly, further suggesting that Fe-CDs facilitated the reaction with H2O2. This makes Fe-CDs a promising candidate for plant applications under abiotic stress conditions. Then, the potential of Fe-CDs nanozyme with multi-enzymes activity in repairing oxidative damage caused by arsenic stress and promoting lettuce growth was investigated. The results indicated that Fe-CDs nanozyme displayed excellent scavenging ROS system ability, enabling lettuce to better cope with oxidative stress induced by arsenic exposure. These findings, therefore, establish a novel platform that elevates CDs nanozyme as a promising substitute for relevant antioxidants in the elimination of ROS.

Fe-CDs were synthesized via hydrothermal synthesis using citric acid, urea and ferric citric as precursors, respectively. Firstly, 1 g citric acid, 0.5 g urea and 3 g ferric citric were dissolved in 30 mL ultrapure water. Subsequently, the mixture was poured into a 50 mL stainless-steel autoclave and kept at a temperature of 230 °C for an additional 4 h. Once cooled to room temperature, the reactants underwent centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min, followed by filtration of the supernatant through a 0.22 µm membrane to eliminate larger particulate matter. Ultimately, the samples were acquired through dialysis (molecular weight cutoff of 500) against ultrapure water for a period of 24 h, followed by freeze-drying. Additionally, CDs were synthesized utilizing citric acid and urea as precursors, employing the aforementioned method.

The morphology and size of Fe-CDs was investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI/Talos L120C). The UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra were obtained by ultraviolet spectrophotometer (UV-2365, Unico, Shanghai) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan). The functional groups of the samples were determined by Fourier Infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet IS10, Thermo Fisher). The elemental components were measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha). Zeta potential measurements of sample were performed using a Zetasizer Nano ZSE (Malvern). Multi-Parameter Analyzer (JPSJ-605F, Leica China) was used to monitor the oxygen production of the samples. Atomic fluorescence spectrometer (AFS-933, Beijing Titian) employed for determining the content of arsenic in lettuce leaves. Laser confocal microscopy (TCS SP8 STED 3X, LEICA) was used to monitor the reactive oxygen species (ROS) within lettuce cells.

The typical natural peroxidase substrate TMB (3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine) was used to assess the POD-like activity of Fe-CDs in acetic acid-sodium acetate(NaAc-HAc) buffered solutions. The specific experimental procedures followed the method of Cui et al. with some modifications [27]. For detailed information, please refer to Supporting Information 2.

The activity of SOD enzyme was qualitatively measured using electron spin resonance (ESR) methodology. In addition, Quantitative analysis of SOD-like activity was indicated through the inhibition rate of O2 •− formation at a series of concentrations of Fe-CDs using the ultraviolet method [28]. Specific methods were presented in Supporting Information, Experimental Section 3.

The CAT-like activity of Fe-CDs was determined using a dedicated oxygen electrode onaMulti-Parameter Analyzer (JPSJ-605F, Leica China) [29]. In a classic test, Fe-CDs was added to H2O2 solution. After that, the oxygen solubility (mg mL−1) was recorded from 0 to 15 min. Please refer to Supporting Information 4 for specific details.

OH was generated via the Fenton reaction with FeSO4 and H2O2. Various concentrations of CDs and Fe-CDs (0–0.8 mg mL−1) were added to remove ·OH. Salicylic acid solution (ethanol-dissolved) was then introduced, as it captures ·OH and exhibited maximum absorption at 510 nm. This allowed for the assessment of ·OH reduction and quantification of removal effect.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were performed on an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E). All of the electrochemical tests were conducted utilizing a well-established standard three-electrode system. For these tests, a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with a precise diameter of 2 mm was employed. which was loaded with CDs and Fe-CDs catalysts, functioning as working electrode in the system. As the reference electrode, a saturated calomel electrode, which was submerged in a saturated KCl solution, was utilized to provide a stable and reproducible reference potential. Additionally, a graphite rod acted as the counter electrode in the setup, using a 5 mM solution of [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− as an electrolyte.

All DFT calculations were performed with Gaussian16, A03 software package [30]. All density functional theory calculations were performed using the B3LYP-D3 functional. The geometry optimization calculations were performed using Stuttgart/Dresden pseudo-potential (SDD) for Fe atom and def2-SVP for other atoms with Polarizable Continuum Model (PCM) for water. Then, the singlet point energy calculations were performed with def2-TZVP for all atoms with Solvation Model Density (SMD) for water. The binding energy was calculated by following equation: E = Ecomplex – (EH2O2 + Es). The density difference was calculated on Multiwfn 3.8 (dev) and drawn by VMD 1.9.3 [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cultivation of Italian lettuce and its treatment under arsenic stress
ce:section.ce:para: The growing of Italian lettuce took place in plant factory and was illuminated with commercial white light. The fully sprouted lettuce seedlings were moved to moist growth cups and subjected to a week of light cultivation at a room temperature of 23 °C. After that, lettuce seedlings with uniform growth were placed on a culture shelf, incubated with Hoagland nutrient solution and continued to grow for 5 days. To determine the role of Fe-CDs in vivo, lettuce was divided into two groups of six plants per group. Fe-CDs was sprayed on the surface of lettuce leaves as control group (distilled water and Fe-CDs). Treated with distilled water or Fe-CDs as a stress group (As and As + Fe-CDs) in a 20 µM arsenic solution. Foliar spraying was performed once a day for 12 days. After 12 days of cultivation on hydroponic board, various physiological indexes and growth parameters were tested and compared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo ROS monitoring via Fe-CDs nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: The accumulation of ROS in vivo in lettuce leaves was detected using 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (H2DCF-DA) and observed through a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). The preparation of the samples involved selecting intact lettuce leaves, followed by slicing them using a cork borer. After that, the lettuce leaves were collected and incubated in ROS dye in TES (2−(tris (hydroxymethyl) methylamino) ethanesulfonic acid) buffer (10 mM, pH 7.5) for 30 min under darkness. Afterwards, the leaves were cleaned with TES buffer and placed onto a microscope slide for imaging via confocal microscopy. Images were captured using the confocal microscope, focusing on selected areas of the mesophyll cells within the leaves. The sample was irradiated at 405 nm light excitation, and then imaging Dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCF) fluorescence signals of Mesophyll cells was taken.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation and structural characterization of Fe-CDs nanozyme with ROS-targeted scavenging system
ce:section.ce:para: Fe-CDs nanozyme were prepared through a one-pot hydrothermal processed involving citric acid, urea, and ferric citrate. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) image showed that Fe-CDs nanozyme were well dispersed in a uniform quasi-spherical shape (Fig. 1 a). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images displayed the lattice spacing of 0.21 ± 0.02 nm, which were assigned to the (100) crystal phases of graphite, suggesting that the Fe-CDs had a graphene-like sp2 conjugate structure [32]. Moreover, the analysis of size distribution distinctly demonstrated that Fe-CDs had an average diameter of 2.01 nm (Fig. 1b). Under ultraviolet irradiation, Fe-CDs solution visually fluoresces blue. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of Fe-CDs, depicted by the red line in Fig. 1c, exhibited electronic transitions occurring approximately at 300 nm. These transitions correspond to n-π* transitions within the surface or molecular state of the Fe-CDs nanozyme. The fluorescence spectra of Fe-CDs present the maximum fluorescence emission spectra (blue line) at 462 nm. While elevating excitation wavelengths, Fe-CDs exhibited excitation dependent FL emission in the range 450 nm to 480 nm (Fig. S1b). Analogously, the spectrum of CDs was excitation-dependent (Fig. S1a), which was a typical attribute of CDs. As excitation wavelength was increased from 300 to 500 nm, the maximum emission undergoes a shiftfrom 491 to 555 nm. This was a common phenomenon caused by surface state emission traps of different energies. The Zeta potential characterization confirmed that Fe-CDs and CDs both have negatively charged surface (−34 mV and −24.5 mV) (Fig. S1c). In contrast, Fe-CDs was more helpful in exerting their ability to enter plant cells as well as being more conducive to their uptake and transport by plants [33].

The FT-IR spectrum presented in Fig. 1d clearly indicated that both CDs and Fe-CDs possessed structures rich in functional groups. Strong stretching vibration of the hydroxyl groups could be obviously observed near 3450 cm−1. Furthermore, the peaks at 1596 cm−1 confirmed the presence of alkyl primary amides in both of them. The peaks observed at 1405 cm−1 and 1064 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO and CN bonds, respectively. The presence of these functional groups suggested that the surface of the materials was hydrophilic and beneficial for biological applications. Notably, compared to CDs, the characteristic peak of FeO at approximately 678 cm−1 appeared in Fe-CDs, generating by the stretching vibration of the metal–ligand bond [34]. Therefore, the FT-IR spectrum indicated the successful synthesis of Fe-CDs nanozyme. The chemical compositions of CDs and Fe-CDs were further investigated using XPS analysis. Fig. S2a showed that CDs was composed of elements C (60.96 %), N (7.5 %) and O (31.54 %). The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. S2b) can be fitted to three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.23 eV and 288.44 eV, corresponding to different types of carbon: CC/CH, CN/CO and CO, respectively. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. S2c) can be fitted to two peaks at 399.7 eV and 401.33 eV, which were attributed to pyrrolic nitrogen (88.13 %), and graphitic nitrogen (11.87 %), respectively. As illustrated in Fig. S2d, the high-resolution O 1s spectrum can be fitted to two peaks at 531.81 eV and 533.01 eV, attributing to CO and CO, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1e, full spectrum of Fe-CDs contained C (77.69 %), N (2.26 %), O (19.55 %) and Fe (0.51 %) elements. The deconvolution displayed of high-resolution XPS short scans of C 1s in Fig. 1f, indicating the multiple C-binding sites. It could be deconvoluted into three peaks, CO at 288.5 eV, CN/CO at 286.4 eV and CC/CH at 284.7 eV, corresponded to the functional groups on the infrared spectrum. It is not difficult to discover that C content of Fe-CDs was higher than that of CDs. Materials with a high carbon content typically have a more optimized electronic structure, which helped to achieve efficient electron transfer in catalytic reactions [35]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1g) could be fitted to two distinctpeaks. One at 399.7 eV, assigned to pyrrolic nitrogen (61.57 %) and another at 401.5 eV, attributed to graphitic nitrogen (38.43 %). The presence of pyrrolic N provided more defects and active sites for material, thus being critical for enzyme catalytic kinetics and catalytic performance. It can be found that the graphitic nitrogen content of Fe-CDs was increased after Fe-doping. Graphitic nitrogen was conducive to accelerating the electron flow between C atoms and improving its catalytic performance [33]. In addition, graphitic nitrogen also formed more active sites, which were conducive to the catalytic reaction [36]. The high-resolution spectrum of the O 1s band, as illustrated in Fig. 1h, wasresolved into CO bond at 533.4 eV and CO bond at 531.8 eV. The Fe 2p XPS spectrum of Fe-CDs was displayed in Fig. 1i, further verifying the presence of Fe. To be specific, the high-resolution of Fe 2p can be fitted to four peaks. The Fe 2p3/2 peak for Fe-CDs could be further decomposed into two binding energies of 711.4 eV and 709.8 eV associated to Fe3+ and Fe2+, respectively. The other two peaks at 716.6 eV and 714.4 eV were ascribed to the satellite peaks. This demonstrated the successful incorporation of both Fe2+ and Fe3+ within Fe-CDs. The Fe2+ and Fe3+ have been established as the catalytic active sites for a range of nanozymes, including POD, SOD and CAT, thereby indicating that Fe-CDs fulfilled the prerequisites of these enzymatic activities. Moreover, Fe3+ was highly oxidizing, which was also the reason why Fe doping endowed Fe-CDs with POD-like activity [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Fe-CDs nanozyme system on the multienzyme-Like activity
ce:section.ce:para: The typical substrates were utilized to analyze the multiple enzyme-mimicking properties exhibited by CDs and Fe-CDs nanozyme. Catalyzed via Fe-CDs, H2O2 converted TMB as a chromogenic substrate to oxidized blue TMB product (ox-TMB) with characteristic absorbance at 652 nm. Both the control group and the CDs group demonstrated insignificant absorbance (Fig. S3a). However, upon the addition of Fe-CDs, a notable change in the absorbance of the sample was observed, confirming that Fe-CDs possessed the ability to efficiently catalyze the oxidation of TMB. Moreover, with the increase of time, the catalytic reaction was enhanced. In the first 60 s, the reaction emerged a good linear relationship (Fig. S3b). When the reaction reached 15 min, there was a plateau (Fig. S4b). These results suggest that Fe doping conferred the ability of Fe-CDs POD-like catalytic reaction. Then, the optimal reaction conditions of Fe-CDs POD-like were further investigated. As the concentration of Fe-CDs increased, the catalytic reaction was enhanced (Fig. S4a) and revealed the best catalytic activity at pH 3 (Fig. S4d). In addition, Fe-CDs nanozyme represented excellent thermal stability, maintaining high enzyme-like activity after 20-80℃ water bath treatment (Fig. S4c).

In the steady-state kinetic assay, the catalytic reactions of Fe-CDs nanozyme were performed under optimized conditions with different concentrations of TMB or H2O2 substrate. Michaelis-Menten model (Fig. 2 a and b) and Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal model were used to calculate the relevant parameters of enzymatic kinetics. The Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) and maximal reaction velocity (Vmax) values were obtained by Michaelis-Menten curves. These values were 8.56 mM and 2.04 × 10−5 M s−1 toward TMB, respectively. Toward H2O2, they were 6.72 mM and 2.82 × 10−5 M s−1, comparable to those of well-studied Fe3O4 nanozyme or the natural horseradish peroxidase (HPR). Compared to previously reported literature, Fe-CDs in this work has higher Vmax than other iron-based materials, which indicated that Fe-CDs possessed excellent POD-like activity level, as shown in Table S1. Fe-CDs had a strong affinity for both TMB and H2O2, and this excellent peroxidase activity was due to the successful doping of Fe and the high electron transfer rate [38].

Subsequently, CAT-mimic nanozymes have been shown to catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into H2O and O2, thereby avoiding the toxic effects of H2O2 on cells. It plays a key role in the biological oxidative stress defense system [15]. The CAT-like activity of CDs and Fe-CDs in the presence of H2O2 were monitored with a dissolved oxygen instrument (room temperature and pH 7.0). Fig. S5e demonstrated the generation of bubbles in an H2O2 solution when CDs or Fe-CDs were added. Interestingly, the O2 generation in Fe-CDs was significantly faster and more frequent than CDs. It was worth noting that catalytic capacity was positively correlated with CDs / Fe-CDs nanozyme concentration and H2O2 concentration. After adding different concentrations of nanozymes, it is obvious that Fe-CDs catalyzed H2O2 to produce oxygen faster than CDs (Fig. S5a and b). Similarly, when adding different concentrations of H2O2 at a certain concentration of nanozymes (Fig. S5c and d), it could be observed that Fe-CDs enabled to catalyze H2O2 decomposition more effectively than CDs. Thus, Fe-CDs unfolded higher CAT-like activity than CDs. Furthermore, Fig. 2c illustrated that the catalytic decomposition of H2O2 by CDs and Fe-CDs followed a typical Michaelis-Menten kinetics. In contrast, the Km constant of Fe-CDs (0.86 mM) was nearly half that of CDs (1.61 mM), indicating that Fe-CDs had a stronger affinity toward H2O2. Additionally, the maximum reaction rate (Vmax) of Fe-CDs was nearly 2.3 times as high as that of CDs. In general, these data mainly supported the claim that Fe doping was essential for H2O2 substrate reduction.

SOD-like activity was assessed using the NBT indicator of O2 •−. Usually, NBT was used to quantitatively assess the inhibitory capacity of CDs and Fe-CDs on O2 •−. The results revealed that the removal efficiency of O2 •− contingent upon the concentration of Fe-CDs nanozymes and SOD-like activity of Fe-CDs was surpassed that of CDs. When the concentration was 0.8 mg mL−1, Fe-CDs was approximately 1.5 times the elimination rate of CDs, demonstrating the importance of iron doping (Fig. 2d). This further confirmed the biological application of Fe-CDs nanozyme in fighting oxidative stress.

In addition, the capability of Fe-CDs to scavenge ·OH was examined. ·OH was produced by the Fenton reaction. The scavenging ability was positively correlated with the concentration of nanozymes (Fig. 2e). Salicylic acid method was used to quantify the ability of nanozymes to remove ·OH [39]. As the concentration of nanozymes increased, the scavenging activity was enhanced. In particular, when the concentration of nanozyme was 0.8 mg mL−1, the ·OH clearance rate of Fe-CDs was 4 times that of CDs, revealing that Fe-CDs nanozyme had excellent ability to scavenging ·OH. Then, the antioxidant capacity of Fe-CDs and CDs was evaluated by measuring its ability to scavenge 2, 2-diphenyl-1-(2, 4, 6-trinitrophenyl) hydrazyl radicals (DPPH·). The addition of CDs and Fe-CDs showed a dose-dependent effect in eliminating DPPH· (Fig. 2f). The experimental results indicated that Fe-CDs had significantly better scavenging effect than CDs. Based on thementionedexperimental results, Fe-CDsnanozymes possessed excellent antioxidant enzymatic activity, effectively eliminating various ROS (such as ·OH, O2 •−, H2O2) and DPPH· free radicals. Taken into together, Fe-CDs was a high efficiency and extensive antioxidant material with great potential for biological applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Fe-CDs nanozyme system on the directional electron transfer
ce:section.ce:para: To further verify the mechanism by which Fe-CDs regulated the ROS system, electron spin resonance (ESR) methodology was employed for qualitative detection of O2 •− and ·OH radicals. As can be seen from Fig. 3 a, the signal component of DMPO/O2 •− dropped sharply in the exist of Fe-CDs and CDs. Especially, Fe-CDs represented better O2 •− scavenging ability than CDs, which was consistent with the NBT method. Additionally, Fenton reaction was employed to generate ·OH, which were then captured using the scavenger DMPO. The ESR spectrum depicting the adducts formed between DMPO/·OH radicals was presented in Fig. 3b. Obviously, the DMPO/·OH signal was decreased in the presence of nanozymes, especially Fe-CDs nanozyme. It is confirmed that Fe-CDs showed a stronger ability to remove free radicals than CDs. It is reported that electron transfer played an important role in scavenging free radicals, and substances with strong electron transfer ability were more likely to react with free radicals. The process of Fe-CDs scavenging free radicals was essentially the process of electron transfer [40]. Fe-CDs exhibited a superior capacity for scavenging free radicals compared to CDs. The reason for this improvement may be attributed to Fe-CDs having a stronger electron transfer ability, which means it can more easily react with free radicals and effectively neutralize their activity by donating or accepting electrons [9]. It can be seen that the enhancement of electron transfer ability was the key factor for the improvement of free radical scavenging ability of Fe-CDs nanozyme. Thus, electrochemical assessments of CDs/Fe-CDs for electron transfer capability were conducted, as shown in Fig. 3f, using a three-electrode system with the electrolyte [Fe(CN)6]4−/[Fe(CN)6]3−. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed and the results were presented in Fig. 3c and d. In comparison to the unmodified electrode (control curve), the peak current observed after modifying the electrode with CDs was notably diminished, attributable to the obstruction of electron transport by the CDs. Subsequently, after modification with Fe-CDs nanozyme, the peak redox current increased, promoting electron transport, which was attributed to the good electron transfer performance of Fe-CDs nanozyme. Besides, it could effectively improve the catalytic activity of nanozyme [41]. These results were highly consistent with those obtained by EIS. Fe-CDs exhibited a smaller semicircle than CDs, which confirmed that a lower charge transfer resistance and allowed for accelerated interfacial charge transfer. The ZView software (a professional electrochemical impedance spectroscopy data analysis software) was used to fitted the impedance diagram to the equivalent circuit (Fig. 3e). Upon analysis, the outcomes demonstrated that the simulated electron transfer resistance of Fe-CDs (177.2 Ω) was significantly lower compared to CDs (189.9 Ω). This reduction underscores the effectiveness of the iron functional potential in mitigating the internal electron transfer resistance within the catalyst. Furthermore, the elevated electron transfer efficiency observed in Fe-CDs proved to be advantageous, facilitating the catalytic decomposition of reactive oxygen species.

To elucidate how does the catalytic site of the nanozyme enhance the enzyme activity, DFT calculations was conducted. These calculations aimed to gain insight into the source of the nanozyme activity exhibited by Fe-doped CDs and the underlying enhancement mechanism. Generally, the electrons transfer can only take place between distance of several atom layers or adjacent atoms. A graphene oxide model consisting of edge-substituted carbon, nitrogen, and carboxylate groups was constructed. To simulate the construction of Fe-doped CDs, an iron ion was utilized to form a coordinate bond with one of the carboxylic acid groups present on the edge of graphene oxide. After structural optimization, the stable structure models of the ordinary CDs nanozyme and Fe-doped CDs nanozyme were obtained triumphantly (Fig. S6a and b). Normally, the absorption energy between the H2O2 molecule and the metal can serve as a convenient descriptor for further study of the main enzyme activity [42]. Therefore, the adsorption process between nanozyme and H2O2 was simulated (Fig. 4 a, b, Fig. S6c and d). On the one hand, the adsorption energy of H2O2 on Fe-CDs was calculated as −0.666 eV, which was significantly lower than that on ordinary CDs (−0.171 eV), demonstrating that the adsorption process of H2O2 on the Fe-CDs nanozyme was more favorable. Due to the strong electro-positivity and the empty d-orbital of iron ion, H2O2 was preferentially adsorbed on the iron ion active site instead of graphene oxide, leading to maximum utilization of surface areas and minimum steric hindrance [43]. On the other hand, the H2O2 molecule adsorbed on CDs had longer bond length than that adsorbed on Fe-CDs. It was speculated that H2O2 adsorbed on iron ion of Fe-CDs was more stable than that adsorbed on N atom in CDs. Moreover, it was also predicted that dissociation of H2O2 can be improved by the weak interaction between the oxygen atoms adsorbed on Fe-CDs [44].

In addition, the difference charge density of adsorption process of H2O2 revealed that the emergence of a broad electron accumulation region appeared between the H2O2 and iron ion active site after the introduction of iron. Here, green area indicated an elevation in electron density, whereas the blue area demonstrated a reduction. Electrons were efficiently transferred from the H2O2 and iron ion active site to the entire graphene oxide plane compared to the insufficient electron dispersion in H2O2-graphene oxide (Fig. 4c and d), suggesting that the introduction of Fe can enhance the charge conductivity so that the enzymatic catalytic activity of Fe-CDs can be improved. Furthermore, the electron transfer number between H2O2 and graphene oxide was calculated as 0.016 e, while the electron transfer number between H2O2 and graphene oxide introduced with iron ion increased to 0.33 e. This suggested that the incorporation of Fe facilitated a more remarkable charge rearrangement between Fe-CDs and H2O2 in favor of high-efficient H2O2 elimination.

Different local charge distributions can be observed on the electrostatic potential (ESP) surface of CDs and Fe-CDs (Fig. 4e–h), indicating the doping of Fe was crucial for the surface charge distribution. The introduction of Fe resulted in a rearrangement of the charges indicated by the catalyst. This caused more electrons to gather near Fe, thus promoting the electronic interaction between Fe and H2O2 [45,46]. Besides, the reactive site presented a state of mutual attraction of positive and negative charges, which manifested the reaction with H2O2. To sum up, findings offered compelling evidence for the origin of Fe-CDs enhanced enzyme catalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Fe-CDs nanozyme application on alleviating effectively arsenic stress in lettuce
ce:section.ce:para: The successful validation of Fe-CDs as POD-like, CAT-like, SOD-like nanozymes, ·OH and DPPH· scavenger prompted us to further explore its practical application for ROS clearance in vivo. Inspired by this, lettuce served asthe plant model to assess the antioxidant protection of Fe-CDs nanozyme under heavy metal arsenic stress. Fe-CDs nanozymes with dosage of 50 mg L−1, 100 mg L−1, 300 mg L−1 was sprayed on hydroponic lettuce daily for 12 days. As shown in Fig. 5 a, no matter with arsenic treatment or not, the growth of lettuce treated with Fe-CDs was significantly better than that of the control. It can be seen that Fe-CDs could enhanced the growth of lettuce. It is worth noting that in the stress group, lettuce treated with Fe-CDs, especially 300 mg L−1 Fe-CDs, could effectively alleviate the toxicity of As. In detail, as shown in Fig. S7a and b, compared with blank group, fresh weight of lettuce leaves and roots increased after spraying with different concentrations of Fe-CDs, and the growth promotion effect was significant. However, the growth of lettuce was inhibited under As stress. Fortunately, the fresh weight of leaves and roots was significantly enhanced by spraying Fe-CDs (Fig. S8a and b). Especially when the concentration of Fe-CDs nanozyme was 300 mg L−1, the fresh weight and dry weight of the leaves were 2.7 times and 2.1 times, respectively, compared to those of the leaves that were not sprayed. The dry weight of leaves and roots in lettuce increased after spraying Fe-CDs (Fig. S7c and d). The same trend also emerged under stress (Fig. S8c and d), which showed that lettuce retained more nutrients after removing water, such as fat, protein, vitamins, and inorganic salts [47,48]. These findings indicated that Fe-CDs nanozyme could effectively promote lettuce growth and alleviate the growth inhibition caused by As stress.

In the control group, chlorophyll concentration, soluble solid content and soluble protein content were generally improved after spraying Fe-CDs nanozyme, indicating that Fe-CDs could promote photosynthesis and improve the nutrient content of lettuce (Fig. S9a–c). Under As-only stress, Chlorophyll and nutrient content decreased significantly. Compared to As stress alone, foliar application of Fe-CDs nanozyme led to a significant increase in nutrient content, almost resembling the state without stress (Fig. S9d–f). Chlorophyll, as a crucial photosynthetic pigment, is capable of capturing light energy during the light-dependent reactions. Notably, after spraying high concentrations (300 mg L−1) of Fe-CDs nanozyme, total chlorophyll concentration and soluble protein increased by about 3 times and 46.9 %, respectively. It indicating that Fe-CDs nanozyme can promote the formation of chlorophyll, thereby enhancing photosynthesis in lettuce. The elevation of soluble proteins played a crucial role in bolstering lettuce's stress tolerance and fostering its growth. Moreover, under stressful condition, Fe-CDs nanozyme sprayed on the leaves significantly increased the soluble solid content of lettuce leaves by 38.09 %, 40.47 % and 47.6 %, respectively. In summary, these data revealed that Fe-CDs nanozyme could not only provide nutrients for lettuce, but also alleviate As stress inhibition.

Being a redox-active metalloid, arsenic frequentlyinduces oxidative stress and acts as a poison in plants. Fortunately, the analysis of arsenic content in lettuce leaves showed that spraying three concentrations of Fe-CDs nanozyme could reduce arsenic content compared with arsenic stress alone (Fig. 5b). However, the content of arsenic in lettuce leaves at concentrations of 50 mg L−1 and 100 mg L−1 Fe-CDs nanozyme had no obvious change. On on hand, compared to 300 mg L−1, 50 mg L−1 and 100 mg L−1 represented low concentrations of carbon dots, and their effectiveness in mitigating arsenic stress was limited. At these low concentrations, Fe-CDs nanozyme may adsorb or complex with partial arsenic ions, but the effect was relatively weak [49,50]. On the other hand, carbon dot treatment encouraged lettuce to form a physical barrier via cell wall thickening and membrane stabilization. However, the effect of low-concentration Fe-CDs in promoting the synthesis or accumulation of certain protective substances on the cell wall and membrane of lettuce was limited. This was insufficient to fully counteract the damage caused by arsenic stress [51]. Therefore, there was no significant difference in reducing arsenic uptake in lettuce between the concentrations of 50 mg L−1 and 100 mg L−1. To be specific, after Fe-CDs (300 mg L−1) nanozyme spraying, As levels in lettuce could be reduced by up to 34.7 %, which elucidated Fe-CDs nanozyme could effectively prevent the uptake of arsenic. This was due to the enhanced stress resistance of lettuce imparted by Fe-CDs nanozyme, achieving relatively normal growth and development via regulating the content of antioxidant substance within the lettuce [52]. After spraying Fe-CDs nanozyme under normal conditions, the total phenol content of lettuce enhanced significantly (Fig. S10b). However, the total phenol content was obviously inhibited under stress, while it rose after treatment with Fe-CDs (Fig. S10a). The augmentation of total phenol could assist lettuce in resisting external stresses and boost the metabolism of lettuce [53]. The antioxidant system of plant serve as crucial indicators for assessing its level of oxidative stress [54]. Thus, the oxidative damage of lettuce was ascertained by means of the MDA content and the activity of antioxidant enzymes. It was reported that MDA content reflected the level of membrane lipid peroxidation and the degree of damage to membrane. The higher the MDA level, the more serious the plant damage [55,56]. Under normal conditions, Fe-CDs spray enabled to reduce MDA levels in lettuce (Fig. S10c). Under arsenic stress, MDA level of lettuce up-regulated, but it could return to normal level with Fe-CDs treatment (decreased by 43.5 %–52.5 %) (Fig. 5c). This suggested that the cells and tissues in the lettuce were less damaged. Besides, changes in antioxidant enzymes can reflect the defense mechanisms that plants may initiate, including the removal of ROS within the plant and the maintenance of redox balance. These mechanisms protect plants from oxidative stress damage and ensure the maintenance of their normal physiological functions and metabolic activities. Specifically speaking, under normal conditions, compared with the lettuce without Fe-CDs, Fe-CDs nanozyme significantly improved the activity of POD, SOD and CAT enzymes (Fig. S10d–f). Among three concentrations, the concentration of 300 mg L−1 showed the best effect, reflecting the excellent antioxidant activity and stress resistance of lettuce. Furthermore, compared with As stress alone, the activity of POD, SOD and CAT enzymes in the stress group was also significantly up-regulated (Fig. 5d–f). As the concentration of Fe-CDs nanozyme increased, POD enzyme activity rose by 104 %, 114 %, and 109 %, respectively. The incorporation of Fe-CDs nanozyme led to a notable elevation in SOD activity, with increased approximately 1.05, 3.63, and 3.04 times the original levels.Furthermore,CAT enzymes exhibited increases of 4.1 %, 60 %, and 62 %, respectively. It is noteworthy that there was no significant difference between concentration of 50 mg L−1 and As stress alone. This may be due to the low concentration of Fe-CDs nanozyme, which did not play a role. Interestingly, on account of the addition of Fe-CDs nanozyme, lettuce was able to remove excessive ROS and relieve oxidative stress caused by As stress. This was consistent with the findings from in vitro experiments. Overall, at a concentration of 300 mg L−1, Fe-CDs nanozyme demonstrated the most effective enhancement on the antioxidant system of lettuce.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Fe-CDs nanozyme application on scavenging effectively excessive ROS
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further explore the effect of Fe-CDs nanozyme on ROS in lettuce. Evaluation of ROS production in mesophyll cells treated with Fe-CDs nanozyme by confocal microscopy imaging with a fluorescent ROS probe DCF (2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein) (Fig. 6 ). Mesophyll cells containing chloroplasts, the main organelle source of ROS in lettuce leaves, were monitored upon As stress. Specifically, lettuce leaves were treated with Fe-CDs nanozyme and water (control), and infiltrated with DCFH-DA ROS probe. After that, the fluorescence intensity of DCF was measured by confocal microscopy. Under 405 nm excitation, mesophyll cells exhibited a weak fluorescence signal under normal conditions (Fig. 6a). As shown in Fig. 6b, lettuce mesophyll emerged a strong fluorescent signal, suggesting that lettuce cells generated a large number of ROS upon As stress. As expected, Fe-CDs nanozyme effectively loweredROS levels in leaf mesophyll under As stress (Fig. 6c). Compared to arsenic stress conditions, the average fluorescence intensity of the leaves decreased by 36 % after Fe-CDs nanozyme treatment. The accumulation level of ROS in the leaves was similar to that of the water control and there was no obvious difference (Fig. 6d). These results revealed that Fe-CDs nanozyme did indeed scavenge ROS system in stressed plants. Normally, the production of ROS in plants and their removal (via antioxidant enzymes) are like two ends of a scale, maintaining a balanced state. However, when stressed, excessive generation of ROS could break out this balance, causing the plant to suffer oxidative stress and destroy the normal growth of the plant. Fortunately, after Fe-CDs nanozyme applying to the leaf surface, Fe-CDs nanozyme was absorbed by the plant. It was conducive to protect the plant by scavenging the ROS in the chloroplast under stress, so that the plant enable to return to the equilibrium state (Fig. 6e) [57]. To sum up, it can be seen that the potential for Fe-CDs nanozyme to be delivered to photosynthetic organelles. And the possible mechanism about iron-doped carbon dots nanozyme for inhibiting effectively the uptake of arsenic stress in lettuce include its antioxidant properties and enzyme activity regulation. First, Fe-CDs nanozyme, leveraging their antioxidant properties, effectively scavenged ROS system generated in lettuce under arsenic stress through efficient electron transfer and enzyme-like catalytic behavior. Besides, it positively affected the activity of antioxidant enzymes related to arsenic uptake in lettuce, thereby enhancing the tolerance of lettuce to arsenic stress.

Fe-CDs nanozyme possessing the function of scavenging ROS system was synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method. In contrast to CDs, Fe-CDs nanozyme exhibited superior multi-mimetic enzymatic activity, resembling the functions of POD, CAT, and SOD enzymes, indicating its potential for diverse applications. This ensures that Fe-CDs effectively scavenged ROS and stabilized free radicals, thereby exhibiting exceptional antioxidant properties and enzyme catalytic efficiency. The key role of iron was confirmed by the DFT theoretical calculation. Specifically, iron served as a catalytic site in Fe-CDs nanozyme, significantly enhancing their catalytic activity. This, in turn, facilitated the adsorption and reaction of H2O2, accelerated electron transfer capabilities, and promoted catalytic efficiency. In biological applications, the high-efficiency enzyme activity of Fe-CDs nanozyme has successfully alleviated the growth inhibition and oxidative damage caused by As stress on lettuce. It is worth noting that this could be attributed to the synergistic effect of various enzyme activities of Fe-CDs nanozyme, which enhanced the stress resistance of lettuce under abiotic stress. Furthermore, the results are of great significance for designing efficient nanozymes to down-regulate ROS and alleviate oxidative stress caused by abiotic stress in biological systems. This research demonstrates that in future work, we can consider developing CDs nanozymes with high catalytic efficiency and low concentration, promoting plant stress resistance and healthy growth under stress circumstances.
